title: Eyes Open, Feet Moving
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Equipment] A small rucksack and weight appropriate to the student's size
[For Mentor]
required.author: Robert Baden-Powell (selected sections) or similar outdoor skills reference

supplementary.title: [For Mentor]
supplementary.author: Patrick Van Horne and Jason A. Riley

competence_markers.description: The student habitually notices environmental details — exits, people, anomalies — without prompting
The student can recall 15+ objects in Kim's Game from a 60-second viewing
The student rucks regularly with age-appropriate load and can sustain a 30+ minute ruck
The student navigates a basic obstacle course with efficient movement and good assessment
The student is comfortable moving in darkness and can describe what their non-visual senses detected
The student can follow a compass bearing and read a simple map to navigate a short route

cross_connections.description: Observation, navigation, rucking, and night movement are all survival skills. This packet builds the physical and perceptual foundation that 2.4 builds on.
Night movement, rucking under load, and obstacle navigation all involve managing discomfort and fear — IC3 training in physical form.
The de-escalation and awareness skills here complement the martial arts foundation. Awareness prevents the fights that martial arts would have to resolve.
Climbing, crawling, carrying, and terrain navigation draw directly on the movement vocabulary from 2.1.1.

content.why_this_matters:
Most people walk through the world without really seeing it. They stare at their phones. They do not notice the people around them. They could not tell you how many exits are in the building they just walked into, or what the person who just passed them looked like, or whether anything seemed unusual in the parking lot.

You are going to be different. You are going to learn to pay attention — really pay attention — to the world around you. Not because you are scared. Not because the world is dangerous around every corner. But because noticing things is a skill, and it is a skill that makes you safer, smarter, and more useful in every situation.

This packet also builds your body for real-world capability. Not gym fitness — the kind of fitness that matters when you need to walk a long way with a heavy pack, navigate through an obstacle course made of logs and rocks, move confidently in the dark, and find your way from one place to another with a map instead of a phone.

The word "tactical" means practical, applied, and useful in real situations. Tactical fitness is not about looking tough. It is about being genuinely capable when the situation is not comfortable, not controlled, and not optional.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The habit of observation** — noticing details in your environment as a default behavior
2. **Kim's Game proficiency** — visual memory and attention training
3. **Rucking as a physical practice** — walking with a weighted pack for fitness and mental toughness
4. **Obstacle navigation** — assessing and moving through physical obstacles in natural terrain
5. **Night movement comfort** — confidence moving in low-light conditions
6. **Basic orienteering** — following a simple map from point A to point B without electronic assistance
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Paying Attention — The Skill Nobody Teaches

Here is a test. The next time you walk into a room — any room — try to answer these questions without looking back:

- How many people were in the room?
- What color was the shirt of the person nearest the door?
- How many exits does the room have?
- Was there anything unusual — anything that did not seem to belong?

Most people cannot answer even one of these questions. Not because they are stupid, but because they were not paying attention. They walked in, found their seat, and focused on what was directly in front of them. Everything else was invisible.

Paying attention is a skill. Like any skill, it gets better with practice and worse without it. You are going to practice it every day until it becomes automatic — until walking into a room and noticing the people, the exits, and anything unusual is something you do without thinking about it.

**Daily observation practice:**

- When you walk into any building — your house, a store, a restaurant, a school — count the exits. Where are the doors? Where are the windows you could get out of? This is not fear. This is awareness.
- When someone walks past you, try to remember three things about them: what they were wearing, how tall they were, which direction they went.
- When you sit down somewhere, look around. What is normal? What would be abnormal? (A backpack sitting by itself with nobody near it. A car parked in a strange place. A person acting differently from everyone around them.)

This is not paranoia. Paranoia is being afraid of everything. Awareness is noticing everything. The difference is important. A person who is aware is calm, curious, and observant. A person who is paranoid is anxious, jumpy, and miserable. You are training awareness — the habit of noticing — not fear.

### Part 2: Kim's Game — Training Your Visual Memory

Kim's Game comes from a book called *Kim* by Rudyard Kipling, written in 1901. In the book, a boy named Kim trains to be a spy by playing this game. It has been used by military and intelligence organizations ever since because it works.

**How to play:**
1. Your mentor places 10–20 small objects on a tray or table — a coin, a key, a button, a small toy, a pencil, a stone, whatever is available.
2. You study the objects for 60 seconds.
3. The tray is covered with a cloth.
4. You write down or describe every object you can remember.

**Start easy (10 objects) and build up.** When you can consistently remember 10, move to 15. When you can remember 15, move to 20. When you can remember 20, your mentor starts removing one object and you must identify which one is missing.

**Why this matters:** Kim's Game trains your brain to notice and remember details. This is the foundational skill beneath all observation — the ability to look at something, really see it, and recall it later. A person who plays Kim's Game regularly for months will notice more and remember more than someone who has never trained this skill.

Play Kim's Game at least once a week. Make it a household tradition.

### Part 3: Rucking — Walking With Weight

Rucking is the simplest and most transferable conditioning method that exists. You put weight in a backpack and walk. That is it. And yet rucking builds leg strength, core stability, cardiovascular endurance, and mental toughness more effectively than most gym exercises — because it is sustained, real-world effort with load.

Every military in the world rucks. Soldiers march with heavy packs over long distances because the ability to move under load is the most fundamental tactical fitness skill. You are starting small, but you are starting.

**Getting started:**
- Pack weight: 10–15% of your bodyweight. If you weigh 60 pounds, that is 6–9 pounds.
- Start with a 15–20 minute walk. Flat ground is fine.
- The pack should sit high on your back, close to your body. Tighten the straps so it does not bounce.
- Walk with good posture — chest up, shoulders back, eyes forward. Do not hunch under the weight.

**Progression:**
- Add 5 minutes to each ruck every week or two
- Gradually increase weight (never more than 20% of bodyweight at this age)
- Add terrain — hills, trails, uneven ground
- Add challenges — navigate to a specific point, carry additional objects in your hands

**The mental component:** Rucking teaches you that you can keep going when you are tired. The pack gets heavy. Your feet get sore. Your shoulders ache. But you keep walking. This is not just fitness — it is the IC3 lesson that discomfort is not an emergency. It is information.

### Part 4: Obstacle Navigation

An obstacle course is a series of physical problems that you solve with your body. A log across the trail — do you go over it or under it? A ditch — can you jump it or do you need to find a way around? A hill — what is the best route up?

**Building an obstacle course:**

You do not need a professional course. You need natural terrain with features:
- A log to climb over or crawl under
- A ditch or stream to cross
- A hill to climb
- A balance beam (fallen tree, low wall)
- An area to crawl through (under bushes, through a low tunnel)
- Something to carry through the course (a sandbag, a heavy rock)

**The skill is assessment:** Before you attack an obstacle, look at it. What is the best approach? Where should you put your hands? Where should you place your feet? What is the risk if you slip? Good obstacle navigation is not just physical — it is the combination of physical capability and problem-solving that produces efficient, safe movement through challenging terrain.

### Part 5: Night Movement — Trusting Your Other Senses

Moving in the dark is uncomfortable for almost everyone, especially at first. Your eyes — which normally provide 80% of your information — become much less useful. You have to rely on your other senses: hearing, touch, balance, and your sense of where your body is in space.

**Night movement practice:**

Start simple. Walk around your yard at dusk, then in full darkness. Walk a familiar trail at night with a headlamp, then without one. Move slowly. Let your eyes adjust — full dark adaptation takes 20–30 minutes. After that, you will be surprised by how much you can see by moonlight or starlight alone.

**What you learn in the dark:**
- You hear more. Sounds that were invisible during the day become obvious at night.
- You feel the ground. You learn to place your feet carefully, to feel the terrain through your shoes, to adjust your balance constantly.
- You manage fear. The darkness is not dangerous by itself. Your discomfort with it is a feeling, not a fact. Learning to move confidently in the dark is IC3 training in a physical package.

### Part 6: Basic Orienteering — Finding Your Way

A compass and a map can get you anywhere. No batteries. No signal needed. No technology that can fail.

**The compass basics:**
- The needle always points north (the red end).
- A bearing is a direction expressed as a number of degrees from north. North is 0 (or 360). East is 90. South is 180. West is 270.
- To follow a bearing: hold the compass flat, rotate the bezel until the desired bearing aligns with the direction-of-travel arrow, then turn your body until the needle aligns with the north mark on the bezel. Walk in the direction the arrow points.

**First exercise:** Your mentor sets a series of bearings and distances: "Walk 100 steps on bearing 90 (east). Then walk 50 steps on bearing 180 (south). Then walk 100 steps on bearing 270 (west). Then walk 50 steps on bearing 360 (north)." You should end up approximately where you started — a rectangle. This exercise teaches bearing-following and pacing.

**Map reading:** A simple trail map or park map. Identify where you are. Identify where you want to go. Trace the route. Then walk it, checking the map as you go. The skill is connecting the lines and symbols on the map to the real terrain around you — the bridge on the map is that bridge ahead. The hill on the map is that hill to your left.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Observation Habit
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously comments on environmental details — people, exits, changes, anomalies. They notice things without being prompted. Kim's Game scores improve steadily.

**Developing slowly:** The student does not notice environmental details without prompting. Kim's Game scores are stagnant. They walk into spaces without scanning.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling awareness regularly? Are you playing Kim's Game weekly? Is the observation log being maintained? This habit builds through consistent practice, not occasional exercises.

### 2. Rucking Capability
**Developing well:** The student rucks 30+ minutes with appropriate load, maintains good posture, and shows mental toughness through discomfort.

**Developing slowly:** The student complains excessively, stops frequently, or shows signs of physical discomfort that suggest the load or duration is too much.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the load appropriate? Is the pack fitting well? Has the progression been too fast? Reduce and rebuild if needed.

### 3. Night Comfort
**Developing well:** The student moves through darkness with confidence, uses non-visual senses effectively, and describes the experience with curiosity rather than fear.

**Developing slowly:** The student refuses to move without a light, shows persistent anxiety in darkness, or does not engage with the experience.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the progression been gradual enough? Start with dusk, not pitch darkness. Fear of the dark is normal at this age and resolves with gradual exposure, not forced immersion.
